findings.title: Token counting on every iteration is O(N) per message, no caching
findings.description: MemoryManager.compact_if_needed() is called EVERY iteration in AgentLoop._run() (line 805). It calls self.count_tokens(msgs) which iterates all messages and calls count_tokens_for_content() on each. With 100+ messages, this recalculates token counts for messages that haven't changed since the last call. There is no incremental token counter or dirty-flag mechanism. This is purely CPU-bound and adds per-iteration latency proportional to message count.
findings.suggestion: Maintain a running token count that increments/decrements as messages are appended. Use a dirty flag to force recompute only when compaction actually modifies the list. Reduces per-iteration cost from O(N) to O(delta).

findings.title: ContextBuilder rebuilds all sources every iteration, including static ones
findings.description: AgentLoop._messages_with_run_context() is called every iteration (line 546 in loop.py). The ContextBuilder.build() re-renders ALL registered sources each call. SystemPromptSource produces identical output every time. AsterMdSource re-reads ASTER.md files from disk every iteration. MemoryIndexSource calls persistent_memory.load_index() every iteration. These static sources produce identical strings across a single run but are recomputed on every loop iteration.
findings.suggestion: Add a build-cache in ContextBuilder keyed by source name + mode. Invalidate only when mode changes or planning/todo state changes. Static sources render once per run() invocation. Use a simple dict with per-source cache keys.

findings.title: RepoMap / SymbolSearch reads and parses files on every tool invocation
findings.description: RepoMapTool and SymbolSearchTool call build_repo_map() which walks the directory tree with os.walk(), reads and parses EVERY file with tree-sitter or Python AST, and constructs a full repo map from scratch on every invocation. For a medium-sized repo with 200+ Python files, this is 200+ file reads and 200+ AST parses per call. The LLM may call RepoMap multiple times in a session but the results are never cached.
findings.suggestion: Add a file-mtime-based cache for RepoMap. Build it once per session, then incrementally update: mark files as dirty when Write/Edit modify them, re-read only dirty files. This turns a 200-file scan into a 0-2 file update on subsequent calls. Alternatively, add a TTL cache with a configurable staleness window (e.g., 30s).

findings.title: No httpx connection pooling / session reuse across LLM calls
findings.description: BaseLLM._get_client() creates a single httpx.AsyncClient for the entire LLM lifetime, which is good. However, the client uses httpx.Timeout(60.0, read=180.0) for non-streaming mode. For streaming SSE, the read timeout is 60s. While this is functional, httpx HTTP/2 support (http2=True) is not enabled, which means each request needs a new TCP+TLS connection if the server supports HTTP/2 multiplexing. Additionally, the httpx client is never warmed up (no initial connection established), so the first API call pays a full TLS handshake penalty.
findings.suggestion: Enable http2=True on the httpx.AsyncClient for servers that support it. Add connection pool parameters (limits=httpx.Limits(max_keepalive_connections=5, max_connections=20)). Consider adding a warm-up HEAD or OPTIONS request during agent initialization to pre-establish the TLS session.

findings.title: CLI main.py imports all heavy dependencies at module level
findings.description: agent/main.py imports typer, dotenv, platformdirs, and then unconditionally imports virtually all agent subsystems at module level: AgentLoop, OpenAILLM, AnthropicLLM, SubAgentManager, build_default_tool_registry, HookManager, SkillRuntime, LspClientManager, TreeSitter, and more. Even for simple 'asterwynd --help', the entire dependency tree is loaded. The typer app uses lazy subcommand imports only for the 'benchmark' command, but 'web' imports uvicorn only inside the function. However, the top-level imports drag in tree_sitter, playwright (via factory.py), and other heavy packages.
findings.suggestion: Move heavy imports inside the functions where they're actually used. Use lazy module-level aliases (e.g., my_function = lambda: import_module('heavy_module').my_function). Benchmark, web, and interactive modes each need different subsets; tree-sitter and playwright are only needed for code intelligence and browser tools respectively. This could reduce 'asterwynd --help' startup time by 50-70%.

findings.title: ContextBuilder uses tiktoken import per-render for token estimation
findings.description: ContextBuilder._apply_budget() calls _estimate_tokens() on every layer, which imports tiktoken.get_encoding('cl100k_base') inside a try/except on every call. While import caching prevents re-importing, the tiktoken encoding creation is called on each budget application. For the context builder, this means every iteration creates a tiktoken encoding instance, even though MemoryManager already has one loaded at module level.
findings.suggestion: Reuse the same _count_tokens function from agent/memory/manager.py (which already caches the tiktoken encoding at module level) instead of creating a separate per-call encoding. Export the counter function and share it.

findings.title: Web server reads index.html from disk on every page request
findings.description: In web/server.py, the chat_page() and debug_page() routes call html_path.read_text(encoding='utf-8') on every HTTP GET request. For a static file that changes only on deployment, this is unnecessary disk I/O.
findings.suggestion: Cache the HTML content in memory at startup (app startup event or module level). Alternatively, serve it via the StaticFiles mount that's already configured at /static.

findings.title: Bash tool uses asyncio.create_subprocess_shell with shell=True
findings.description: SandboxExecutor.run() always uses asyncio.create_subprocess_shell() with shell=True. This spawns an intermediate /bin/sh process for every command, adding ~1ms latency and a shell process. For simple commands (e.g., 'ls', 'cat'), using subprocess_exec without a shell would be faster and more secure. Additionally, the pipe limit is set to 1MB (limit=1024*1024), which can cause memory pressure for commands that produce large output.
findings.suggestion: Detect whether the command contains shell operators (|, &&, >, <, $, etc.) and only use shell=True when needed. For simple commands, use subprocess_exec with program arguments. Also consider streaming large output in chunks instead of buffering entirely in memory.

findings.title: MemoryManager.compact() calls LLM for summarization synchronously during agent loop
findings.description: When LLMSummarizer is used, compact() makes a synchronous LLM call to generate a summary. This LLM call blocks the agent loop iteration that triggered it. Since compaction is triggered when ~90% of the token budget is reached, this can add significant latency (2-10 seconds for a summarization call) right when the agent is deep in a task. The compaction runs inline rather than as a background task. Additionally, the LLM summarizer sends the ENTIRE conversation text as a user message, which can be extremely large and may itself exceed token limits.
findings.suggestion: Run summarization as a background coroutine (create_task) that completes before the next iteration. If not ready by the next iteration, use the simple truncation strategy and replace with the summary when ready. Also add a max character limit to _format_messages_for_summary to prevent the summarizer prompt from exceeding context windows.

findings.title: GrepTool reads entire file contents into memory for regex matching
findings.description: GrepTool reads each file with file.read_text().splitlines(), which loads the entire file into memory, creates a list of all lines, then iterates them with regex.search(). For a 50MB log file, this reads 50MB into memory even if only the first line matches. The search is also single-threaded, iterating files sequentially in a for loop.
findings.suggestion: Use mmap or line-by-line streaming with a file iterator. Read one line at a time: for line in f: if regex.search(line): ... . Only load the entire file if the match count needs to be known (e.g., for the 'more matches' footer). For large directories, consider using asyncio for parallel file reading.

findings.title: Benchmark runner uses asyncio.Semaphore for parallelism but subprocess calls use asyncio.to_thread
findings.description: BenchmarkRunner uses asyncio.Semaphore for concurrency control and asyncio.gather for parallel execution. However, all git operations, file I/O, and test execution use asyncio.to_thread() or blocking subprocess.run() within to_thread, which creates OS threads. With --parallel 4, each task creates its own git worktree (subprocess.run), then runs an agent (potentially making many LLM API calls), then runs test commands (subprocess.run). The thread pool default is limited (usually min(32, os.cpu_count()+4)). The combination of parallel agent runners each making async LLM calls plus blocking subprocess calls in threads can lead to thread starvation.
findings.suggestion: For git operations within async context, use asyncio.create_subprocess_exec instead of subprocess.run via to_thread. Set an explicit thread pool executor with a larger max_workers for benchmark scenarios. Consider using ProcessPoolExecutor for CPU-bound git operations.

findings.title: LspClientManager uses threading.Lock for async code
findings.description: LspClientManager.get_client_for_file() uses threading.Lock (self._lock = threading.Lock()) even though the manager is used in an async context. While fine for a single-agent scenario, if multiple coroutines access the LSP manager concurrently, this threading.Lock will block the event loop thread, causing all async tasks to stall.
findings.suggestion: Replace threading.Lock with asyncio.Lock. This ensures the event loop isn't blocked when multiple coroutines need to create LSP clients concurrently.

findings.title: ASTER.md files re-read from disk on every context build
findings.description: AsterMdSource.render() walks the directory chain from git root to CWD every iteration, calling Path.is_file() and file_path.read_text() for each ASTER.md and ASTER.local.md found. For a deep directory tree with multiple ASTER.md files, this is redundant filesystem access on every single agent iteration.
findings.suggestion: Cache the rendered ASTER.md content at render time with a hash of the file paths and their mtimes. Only re-read when a file's mtime changes. This eliminates redundant I/O for the vast majority of iterations.

findings.title: Tool call results not truncated before being added to message history
findings.description: In AgentLoop._run(), tool results are appended directly to the messages list (line 798) without size-based truncation. If a Read tool returns 100KB of file content, or a Bash tool returns a large command output, that entire result becomes part of the message history and will be sent to the LLM in every subsequent API call. This grows the context window unnecessarily quickly and triggers more frequent and expensive compactions.
findings.suggestion: Add configurable truncation of tool results before appending to message history: truncate to N characters with a note like '[output truncated, N chars shown of M total]'. Store the full result separately for recovery if needed.

findings.title: AgentLoop creates new BuildContext with os.getcwd() every iteration
findings.description: In _messages_with_run_context(), a new BuildContext is created every iteration with cwd=os.getcwd(). While os.getcwd() is a fast syscall, it's called once per agent loop iteration unnecessarily since the working directory rarely changes during a run.
findings.suggestion: Cache cwd in AgentLoop.__init__ or resolve once per run() invocation. The working directory is stable during a run.

findings.title: tiktoken import at module level in memory/manager.py blocks startup
findings.description: agent/memory/manager.py does a try/except ImportError for tiktoken at module level (lines 15-22), creating a module-level _enc object. tiktoken is installed as a dependency, so this import always happens. tiktoken's cl100k_base encoding loads a ~1MB tokenizer file from disk, which adds to import time. This import chain is triggered whenever agent/loop.py is imported (since loop.py imports MemoryManager).
findings.suggestion: Move tiktoken encoding creation to a lazy accessor (e.g., _get_encoding() that caches on first call). While the module import is quick, the tokenizer file load is not. Lazily initializing it avoids paying the cost on 'asterwynd --help' paths.

findings.title: No ConnectionError/retry logic for httpx failures in LLM calls
findings.description: Both AnthropicLLM and OpenAILLM call client.post() but neither implements retry logic for transient network errors (ConnectionError, RemoteProtocolError, ReadTimeout). The RetryHook only applies to tool execution, not LLM API calls. A single network blip causes the entire agent run to fail with an unhandled exception.
findings.suggestion: Add httpx retry transport or an outer retry wrapper in BaseLLM for transient errors (HTTP 429, 502, 503, connection errors). Use exponential backoff with jitter. The httpx-retry or tenacity library can provide this with minimal code.

findings.title: Streaming LLM accumulator creates many small string concatenations
findings.description: In AnthropicLLM._stream_chat_impl() (line 223), text_content += text_delta does string concatenation in a loop. In Python, string concatenation in a loop is O(N^2) because each + creates a new string. For very long streaming responses (16K tokens), this can cause measurable memory churn. The append-to-list pattern used in _build_response already fixes this for the final output, but the intermediate text_content used for streaming yield is recomputed every chunk.
findings.suggestion: Use a list for accumulating stream chunks: content_parts = []; content_parts.append(text_delta); then ''.join(content_parts) for the final value. The agent/loop.py already uses a similar pattern in _call_llm (line 843).

findings.title: Session save is synchronous during AgentLoop.run() cleanup
findings.description: In AgentLoop.run() (lines 459-463), session saving is called in the finally block. While wrapped in try/except, _save_session constructs a SessionSnapshot including list(messages) (a full copy), and SessionStore.save writes to disk. This happens before the run result is returned, blocking the caller on disk I/O.
findings.suggestion: Fire-and-forget the session save using asyncio.create_task() so it doesn't block the run() return. If the save is critical, ensure the task is awaited at shutdown.

summary: Performance review of Asterwynd identified 20 findings (5 high, 9 medium, 6 low). The highest-impact issues are: (1) missing caching layers at every level -- context building, token counting, repo map parsing are all recomputed every iteration from scratch; (2) static sources (system prompt, ASTER.md) re-read from disk on every agent loop iteration; (3) repo map re-parses all files on every tool invocation; (4) heavy top-level imports slow CLI startup for all commands including --help. Quick wins include adding a context build cache (saves ~100ms/iteration), caching token counts incrementally (saves O(N)/iteration), and lazy-loading heavy dependencies (saves ~200-500ms startup). The architecture is fundamentally sound with proper async usage for LLM calls, good parallel tool execution via asyncio.gather, and reasonable timeout handling.
